1:
"Forasmuch then as Christ hath suffered for us in the flesh, arm yourselves likewise with the same mind: for he that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin;" The witnesses note the call to arm with the mind of Christ, who suffered in the flesh. On "Christ hath suffered for us in the flesh, arm yourselves likewise with the same mind," the grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note the inference from Christ's suffering (3:18): "Christ hath suffered for us in the flesh (in his body), arm yourselves likewise with the same mind (the same purpose, resolve, disposition)" — as soldiers take up weapons, believers are to arm themselves with the same resolute mind Christ had — a readiness to suffer in doing God's will.

On "arm yourselves... with the same mind," the witnesses note the resolve. Barnes and Calvin note that the believer is to be armed with the determination to suffer rather than sin, the same mind that carried Christ through his suffering; the figure of a warrior taking up arms for the conflict.

On "he that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin," interpreters note the meaning. Barnes and Calvin note two senses: either that one who has suffered for righteousness has, by that very willingness, broken with sin (the resolve to suffer rather than sin means one has done with sin); or that as Christ's suffering ended his connection with sin-bearing, so the believer who in union with Christ suffers (dies to the flesh) has ceased from sin. The verse displays the call to arm with the mind of Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "Christ hath suffered... in the flesh, arm yourselves... with the same mind (resolve)," arming with Christ's resolute mind, ready to suffer in doing God's will (Barnes, Robertson); the determination to suffer rather than sin, like a warrior armed (Barnes, Calvin); "he that hath suffered in the flesh hath ceased from sin," the willingness to suffer breaking with sin, or dying to the flesh ending sin (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the armed mind. The verse marks the suffering: Christ's, in the flesh. The verse displays the arming: the same mind. The verse displays the resolve: to suffer not sin. The verse displays the result: ceased from sin. The call displays the believer armed with Christ's suffering resolve. The verse displays the Christ: suffered. The verse displays the mind: armed. The verse displays the sin: ceased. The call to arm with the mind of Christ, who suffered in the flesh, displays the soldier's summons of the chapter: since Christ suffered in the flesh, the believer is to take up the same resolute mind — ready to suffer rather than sin — for he who has so suffered in the flesh has done with sin. The verse displays the armed resolve, lived no longer to lust but to God (v.2).
2:
"That he no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh to the lusts of men, but to the will of God." The brief verse notes the new aim: to live to God's will, not to men's lusts. The grammatical voices note the purpose of the armed mind. Barnes and Robertson note the goal: "that he no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh (the remainder of his earthly life) to the lusts of men (human passions, sinful desires), but to the will of God" — the purpose of arming with Christ's mind (v.1) is that the believer spend the rest of his life governed not by sinful human desires but by God's will.

On "no longer... to the lusts of men," the witnesses note the break with the past. Barnes and Calvin note the decisive change: the believer is done with living for sinful passions; the old life of lust is left behind; "no longer" marks the break.

On "but to the will of God," the grammatical-historical note the new master: "but to the will of God" — the remaining life is to be governed by God's will as its rule and aim; obedience to God replaces obedience to lust; the transformed direction of the Christian life. The verse displays the new aim: to live to God's will, not to men's lusts.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the goal — "no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh to the lusts of men, but to the will of God," the rest of life governed not by sinful desires but by God's will (Barnes, Robertson); "no longer... to the lusts of men," the decisive break with the old life of lust (Barnes, Calvin); "but to the will of God," God's will the new rule and aim, obedience replacing lust (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the new aim. The verse marks the time: the rest of life. The verse displays the rejection: the lusts of men. The verse displays the new rule: the will of God. The verse displays the change: decisive. The aim displays the life turned from lust to God's will. The verse displays the lusts: forsaken. The verse displays the will: of God. The verse displays the living: transformed. The new aim — to live to God's will, not to men's lusts — displays the purpose of the armed mind: that the believer spend the remainder of his earthly life no longer governed by sinful human desires but by the will of God. The verse displays the transformed life, breaking with the heathen past (v.3).
3:
"For the time past of our life may suffice us to have wrought the will of the Gentiles, when we walked in lasciviousness, lusts, excess of wine, revellings, banquetings, and abominable idolatries:" The witnesses note that the past life of heathen sin is enough. On "the time past of our life may suffice us to have wrought the will of the Gentiles," the grammatical voices note the ironic sufficiency. Barnes and Robertson note the pointed statement: "the time past of our life may suffice (is enough) us to have wrought (done, carried out) the will of the Gentiles (the heathen, the unconverted)" — the past time spent in heathen sin is more than enough; there is no need to give any more of life to such ways; ironic understatement.

On the catalogue of sins, the witnesses note the heathen vices. Barnes and Calvin note the list: "lasciviousness (sensuality), lusts (evil desires), excess of wine (drunkenness), revellings (carousing), banquetings (drinking parties)" — the characteristic sins of the pagan life, especially sensuality and intemperance; the works of the flesh the readers had practised before conversion.

On "abominable idolatries," the grammatical-historical note the climax: "abominable (unlawful, detestable) idolatries" — the crowning sin, the worship of idols, often bound up with the immoral revelry just named; the whole catalogue marks the corrupt life from which the gospel had delivered them. The verse displays that the past life of heathen sin is enough.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ironic sufficiency — "the time past... may suffice (is enough) us to have wrought the will of the Gentiles," the past spent in heathen sin more than enough, no more life to be given to it (Barnes, Robertson); the catalogue "lasciviousness, lusts, excess of wine, revellings, banquetings," the characteristic pagan vices of sensuality and intemperance (Barnes, Calvin); "abominable idolatries," the crowning sin of idol-worship (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the heathen past. The verse marks the time: past, enough. The verse displays the will: of the Gentiles. The verse displays the vices: sensuality, drink. The verse displays the climax: idolatries. The catalogue displays the corrupt past the gospel delivered them from. The verse displays the past: sufficient. The verse displays the sins: heathen. The verse displays the idolatry: abominable. That the past life of heathen sin is enough displays the appeal to break with the old ways: the time already spent doing the will of the heathen — in sensuality, lust, drunkenness, carousing, and abominable idolatry — is more than enough; no more of life should be given to such things. The verse displays the renounced past, which the heathen find strange (v.4).
4:
"Wherein they think it strange that ye run not with them to the same excess of riot, speaking evil of you:" The witnesses note that the heathen malign believers who no longer join their excess. On "they think it strange that ye run not with them," the grammatical voices note the surprise. Barnes and Robertson note the reaction of the former companions: "they think it strange (are surprised, find it odd) that ye run not with them to the same excess of riot (the same flood of dissipation, the wild rush of profligacy)" — the heathen are astonished that the believers no longer plunge with them into the same excesses; the converted have broken from the crowd.

On "the same excess of riot," the witnesses note the dissipation. Barnes and Calvin note that "excess of riot (asotia — profligacy, debauchery)" is the wild, reckless dissipation of the pagan life; the believers' refusal to join it marks them out and provokes resentment.

On "speaking evil of you," the puritan Henry notes the slander: the heathen, surprised and offended, respond by "speaking evil of you (slandering, reviling)" — they malign and slander the Christians for their separation; the godly life rebukes the wicked, who retaliate with slander; the believer must expect such evil-speaking. The verse displays that the heathen malign believers who no longer join their excess.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the surprise — "they think it strange that ye run not with them to the same excess of riot (dissipation)," the heathen astonished the believers no longer plunge into excess (Barnes, Robertson); "excess of riot," the wild reckless dissipation the believers refuse, marking them out (Barnes, Calvin); "speaking evil of you," the offended heathen slandering the Christians for their separation (Henry). The verse displays the slander. The verse marks the surprise: think it strange. The verse displays the refusal: run not with them. The verse displays the excess: of riot. The verse displays the response: speaking evil. The reaction displays the heathen maligning the separated believers. The verse displays the strange: thought. The verse displays the riot: refused. The verse displays the evil: spoken. That the heathen malign believers who no longer join their excess displays the cost of separation: the former companions, astonished that the believers no longer rush with them into dissipation, respond by slandering them — the godly separation provoking the world's resentment and evil-speaking. The verse displays the slandering heathen, who must give account to the Judge (v.5).
5:
"Who shall give account to him that is ready to judge the quick and the dead." The brief verse notes that the slanderers must give account to the Judge of living and dead. The grammatical voices note the coming reckoning. Barnes and Robertson note the answer to the slander: "who shall give account (a reckoning) to him that is ready (prepared, at hand) to judge the quick (the living) and the dead" — the heathen slanderers will have to answer to God (or Christ), who is ready to judge all, both the living and the dead; their evil-speaking will be reckoned with.

On "ready to judge," the witnesses note the imminence. Barnes and Calvin note that the Judge is "ready" — prepared and at hand to judge; the judgment is certain and near; the slanderers should remember they face a Judge.

On "the quick and the dead," the grammatical-historical note the universal judgment: "the quick and the dead" — all without exception, both those alive at the judgment and those already dead; the comprehensive scope of the judgment is the comfort of the persecuted (their slanderers will answer) and a solemn warning. The verse displays that the slanderers must give account to the Judge of living and dead.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reckoning — "who shall give account to him that is ready to judge the quick (living) and the dead," the slanderers answering to the Judge of all (Barnes, Robertson); "ready to judge," the Judge prepared and at hand, the judgment certain and near (Barnes, Calvin); "the quick and the dead," the universal scope, comforting the persecuted and warning the wicked (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the coming judgment. The verse marks the account: to be given. The verse displays the Judge: ready. The verse displays the scope: quick and dead. The verse displays the comfort: slanderers answer. The reckoning displays the slanderers facing the universal Judge. The verse displays the account: required. The verse displays the judging: ready. The verse displays the dead: included. That the slanderers must give account to the Judge of living and dead displays the answer to the world's evil-speaking: those who slander the believers will themselves give account to God, who is ready to judge both the living and the dead — the certain reckoning that comforts the persecuted. The verse displays the universal Judge, before whom even the dead were evangelized (v.6).
6:
"For for this cause was the gospel preached also to them that are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit." The witnesses note the gospel preached to the dead — a difficult and contested crux. On the meaning of "them that are dead," interpreters divide. The grammatical-historical Barnes (with Wetstein, Rosenmuller, Bloomfield) holds "the dead" are literally those now dead who heard the gospel while living and became Christians: the gospel was preached to them (when alive) so that, though "judged according to men in the flesh" (condemned and put to death by men, as the martyrs were), they might "live according to God in the spirit" (live to God in their souls, both now and forever) — an encouragement to persecuted believers from the example of the faithful dead. A second view (Doddridge, Whitby, Augustine, Luther) takes "the dead" as the spiritually dead — the Gentiles, dead in sins, to whom the gospel was preached. A third (Clarke, Macknight, Wesley) links it to 3:19: "the dead" are the antediluvians, to whom Christ preached by Noah, that though condemned in the flesh by the flood they might live to God in the spirit. A fourth (Ellicott, Calvin) takes it of the literal dead to whom Christ preached in hades (referring back to 3:19), the gospel penetrating even to the dead.

On "judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note the antithesis: "judged (condemned, put to death) according to men (so far as men are concerned) in the flesh, but live according to God (so far as God is concerned) in the spirit" — though men condemn and kill the body, God gives the soul true life; the flesh suffers, the spirit lives.

The witnesses agree the substance: the gospel was preached "to the dead" with the result that, though judged/condemned in the flesh (by men, or by the flood), they live to God in the spirit; the contrast of flesh and spirit, death and life, is the comfort. But who "the dead" are divides them: now-dead believers (Barnes), the spiritually dead Gentiles (Doddridge), the antediluvians preached to by Noah (Clarke, Wesley), or the literal dead preached to in hades (Ellicott, Calvin, linking to 3:19). The verse displays the gospel-to-the-dead crux. The verse marks the preaching: to the dead. The verse displays the debate: now-dead believers, spiritually dead, antediluvians, or the dead in hades. The verse displays the contrast: judged in flesh, live in spirit. The verse displays the comfort: life with God. The crux displays the gospel reaching the dead with the promise of life. The verse displays the gospel: preached. The verse displays the dead: evangelized. The verse displays the spirit: living. The gospel preached to the dead displays a crux bound up with 3:19: the gospel was preached "to them that are dead," that though judged in the flesh they might live to God in the spirit — read as the now-dead believers who suffered for the faith (Barnes), the spiritually dead Gentiles (Doddridge), the antediluvians preached to by Noah (Clarke, Wesley), or the literal dead reached in hades (Ellicott, Calvin). The verse displays the dead made alive to God, as the end of all things nears (v.7).
7:
"But the end of all things is at hand: be ye therefore sober, and watch unto prayer." The witnesses note that the nearness of the end calls for sobriety and watchful prayer. On "the end of all things is at hand," the grammatical voices note the imminence. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn declaration: "the end of all things is at hand (has drawn near, is approaching)" — the consummation of all things is near; whether the end of the Jewish state, the end of each man's life, or the final consummation, the nearness of the end is the ground for the exhortations that follow.

On "be ye therefore sober," the witnesses note the call to sobriety. Barnes and Calvin note that "sober (sophronesate — sound-minded, self-controlled, serious)" — the nearness of the end calls for a sober, self-controlled mind, not given to excess or frivolity; clear-headed seriousness in view of the coming consummation.

On "watch unto prayer," the puritan Henry notes the watchfulness in prayer: "watch (be vigilant, alert) unto prayer" — sobriety is to issue in watchful prayer; the believer is to be vigilant and prayerful, not drowsy or distracted, keeping alert for prayer in light of the end. The verse displays that the nearness of the end calls for sobriety and watchful prayer.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the imminence — "the end of all things is at hand (has drawn near)," the consummation near, the ground for the exhortations (Barnes, Robertson); "be ye therefore sober (self-controlled, serious)," the nearness calling for a sober, clear-headed mind (Barnes, Calvin); "watch unto prayer," sobriety issuing in vigilant, alert prayer (Henry). The verse displays the call to sobriety. The verse marks the end: at hand. The verse displays the sobriety: sound-minded. The verse displays the watching: alert. The verse displays the prayer: vigilant. The exhortation displays the near end calling for sober, watchful prayer. The verse displays the end: near. The verse displays the sober: enjoined. The verse displays the prayer: watched unto. That the nearness of the end calls for sobriety and watchful prayer displays the practical force of the consummation: since the end of all things is at hand, the believer is to be sober and self-controlled, and to watch vigilantly unto prayer. The verse displays the watchful sobriety, crowned by fervent love (v.8).
8:
"And above all things have fervent charity among yourselves: for charity shall cover the multitude of sins." The witnesses note the call to fervent love, which covers a multitude of sins. On "above all things have fervent charity among yourselves," the grammatical voices note the supreme grace. Barnes and Robertson note the emphasis: "above all things (before all, chiefly) have fervent (ektene — earnest, intense, stretched-out) charity (love) among yourselves" — love is the chief grace, to be cultivated above all; "fervent" pictures love strained to the utmost, intense and earnest.

On "fervent charity," the witnesses note the intensity. Barnes and Calvin note that the love is to be "fervent" — not cool or formal but warm, earnest, and persevering; the strongest mutual love among believers.

On "charity shall cover the multitude of sins," the puritan Henry notes the reason. Barnes and Calvin note the sense (Proverbs 10:12): "charity shall cover the multitude of sins" — love covers, overlooks, forgives, and conceals the faults of others; not by atoning for sin, but by being ready to pardon and not expose the many offences of the brethren; love throws a veil over wrongs rather than publishing them. The verse displays the call to fervent love, which covers a multitude of sins.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the supreme grace — "above all things have fervent (earnest, intense) charity among yourselves," love the chief grace, strained to the utmost (Barnes, Robertson); "fervent," warm, earnest, persevering love (Barnes, Calvin); "charity shall cover the multitude of sins" (Proverbs 10:12), love forgiving and concealing the faults of others, throwing a veil over wrongs (Henry). The verse displays fervent love. The verse marks the priority: above all. The verse displays the love: fervent. The verse displays the effect: covers sins. The verse displays the manner: forgiving. The call displays love as the supreme, sin-covering grace. The verse displays the charity: fervent. The verse displays the sins: covered. The verse displays the love: chief. The call to fervent love, which covers a multitude of sins, displays the chief of the graces: above all, believers are to have earnest, intense love for one another — for love covers a multitude of sins, ready to forgive and conceal the faults of the brethren rather than expose them. The verse displays the covering love, shown in ungrudging hospitality (v.9).
9:
"Use hospitality one to another without grudging." The brief verse notes the call to ungrudging hospitality. The grammatical voices note the duty of love in action. Barnes and Robertson note the practical expression of love: "use hospitality (be hospitable, welcome and lodge strangers and fellow-believers) one to another" — love issues in hospitality, the welcoming and entertaining of others, especially needful when travelers and persecuted believers needed lodging.

On "hospitality," the witnesses note its importance. Barnes and Calvin note that hospitality was a vital duty in the early church — believers travelling or fleeing persecution depended on the open homes of fellow-Christians; a concrete fruit of the fervent love just commanded.

On "without grudging," the puritan Henry notes the spirit: hospitality is to be shown "without grudging (without murmuring, complaint, or reluctance)" — gladly and willingly, not grudgingly or with a sense of burden; the spirit of cheerful, open generosity, not mere reluctant duty. The verse displays the call to ungrudging hospitality.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the duty — "use hospitality (welcome and lodge others) one to another," love issuing in hospitality, welcoming travelers and believers (Barnes, Robertson); hospitality a vital early-church duty, the open home for travelling and persecuted Christians (Barnes, Calvin); "without grudging," gladly and willingly, not reluctantly or with complaint (Henry). The verse displays the hospitality. The verse marks the duty: hospitality. The verse displays the mutuality: one to another. The verse displays the spirit: without grudging. The verse displays the manner: cheerful. The call displays love opening the home gladly. The verse displays the hospitality: practiced. The verse displays the grudging: avoided. The verse displays the welcome: glad. The call to ungrudging hospitality displays a concrete fruit of fervent love: believers are to welcome and lodge one another — gladly and willingly, without murmuring or reluctance. The verse displays the cheerful hospitality, part of faithful stewardship of grace (vv.10-11). The placing of this duty here is deliberate: fervent love (v.8) is not to remain an inward sentiment but to issue in concrete acts of welcome, and the added words without grudging guard against the subtle failure of doing the deed while begrudging the cost, which would empty the act of its love. Hospitality grudgingly given wounds rather than helps; only the glad and open hand truly ministers the grace of God to the guest.
10:
"As every man hath received the gift, even so minister the same one to another, as good stewards of the manifold grace of God." The witnesses note the call to use one's gift as a good steward of God's grace. On "as every man hath received the gift, even so minister the same one to another," the grammatical voices note the duty of service. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "as every man hath received the gift (charisma — a gift of grace, whatever endowment God has given), even so minister (serve with) the same one to another" — each believer has received some gift, and is to use it in serving others; the gift is for ministry, not self.

On "every man hath received the gift," the witnesses note the universality. Barnes and Calvin note that every believer has received some gift — none is without a gift to use; the variety of gifts is distributed among all, each for the good of the body.

On "as good stewards of the manifold grace of God," the grammatical-historical note the stewardship: believers are "good stewards (faithful managers, trustees) of the manifold (poikiles — varied, many-coloured) grace of God" — the gifts are not the believer's own but God's grace entrusted to him; he is a steward, accountable to use the varied grace faithfully for others. The verse displays the call to use one's gift as a good steward of God's grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "as every man hath received the gift, even so minister the same one to another," each using his gift in serving others, the gift for ministry (Barnes, Robertson); every believer having received some gift, none without one, the variety for the body's good (Barnes, Calvin); "good stewards of the manifold (varied) grace of God," the gifts God's grace entrusted, to be managed faithfully (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the stewardship. The verse marks the gift: received by each. The verse displays the ministry: to one another. The verse displays the stewardship: good. The verse displays the grace: manifold. The call displays each gift used faithfully for others. The verse displays the gift: received. The verse displays the ministering: mutual. The verse displays the steward: faithful. The call to use one's gift as a good steward of God's grace displays the duty of mutual service: as each believer has received some gift, he is to use it in serving others — a faithful steward of the varied grace of God entrusted to him. The verse displays the faithful stewardship, exercised in speech and service to God's glory (v.11).
11:
"If any man speak, let him speak as the oracles of God; if any man minister, let him do it as of the ability which God giveth: that God in all things may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom be praise and dominion for ever and ever. Amen." The witnesses note the call to speak and serve so that God may be glorified. On "if any man speak, let him speak as the oracles of God," the grammatical voices note the duty in speaking. Barnes and Robertson note the rule for those who teach: "if any man speak (in teaching or ministry), let him speak as the oracles (logia — the very utterances, sayings) of God" — the one who speaks in the church is to speak as one delivering God's own words, with reverence and fidelity, not his own opinions.

On "if any man minister, let him do it as of the ability which God giveth," the witnesses note the duty in serving. Barnes and Calvin note the rule for practical service: "if any man minister (serve, in deeds of help), let him do it as of the ability (strength) which God giveth (supplies)" — the one who serves is to do so in the strength God provides, acknowledging that even the power to serve is God's gift.

On "that God in all things may be glorified... to whom be praise and dominion for ever," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the end and doxology: the aim of all is "that God in all things may be glorified through Jesus Christ" — God's glory the supreme end of every gift and service; and the verse closes with a doxology, "to whom be praise (glory) and dominion for ever and ever. Amen." The verse displays the call to speak and serve so that God may be glorified.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: speaking — "if any man speak, let him speak as the oracles (very utterances) of God," speaking God's words with reverence, not one's own opinions (Barnes, Robertson); serving — "as of the ability which God giveth," serving in the strength God supplies (Barnes, Calvin); the end "that God in all things may be glorified through Jesus Christ," God's glory the supreme aim, closing in doxology (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the glorifying service. The verse marks the speaking: as God's oracles. The verse displays the serving: in God's strength. The verse displays the end: God glorified. The verse displays the doxology: praise for ever. The call displays gift and service aimed at God's glory. The verse displays the oracles: spoken. The verse displays the ability: God's. The verse displays the glory: God's. The call to speak and serve so that God may be glorified displays the governing aim of all gifts: the one who speaks is to speak as God's own oracles, and the one who serves in the strength God supplies — that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom be praise and dominion forever. The verse displays the doxology, opening the call not to be surprised by fiery trial (v.12).
12:
"Beloved, think it not strange concerning the fiery trial which is to try you, as though some strange thing happened unto you:" The witnesses note the call not to be surprised by the fiery trial. On "think it not strange concerning the fiery trial," the grammatical voices note the expected suffering. Barnes and Robertson note the tender exhortation: "beloved, think it not strange (be not surprised, do not regard as alien) concerning the fiery trial (pyrosis — the burning, the ordeal by fire) which is to try (test, prove) you" — the persecution about to come upon them, like a refiner's fire, is not to be thought strange or unexpected; trial is the normal lot of the believer.

On "the fiery trial which is to try you," the witnesses note the testing. Barnes and Calvin note that the "fiery trial" is the severe persecution that tests and proves faith, as fire assays metal; it is sent to prove and purify, not to destroy.

On "as though some strange thing happened unto you," the puritan Henry notes the right expectation: believers are not to react "as though some strange (foreign, unheard-of) thing happened" — suffering for Christ is normal, foretold, and to be expected; to be surprised by it would betray a wrong understanding of the Christian calling. The verse displays the call not to be surprised by the fiery trial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exhortation — "think it not strange (be not surprised) concerning the fiery trial (the ordeal by fire) which is to try (prove) you," the coming persecution not to be thought strange, trial the normal lot (Barnes, Robertson); the "fiery trial" the severe persecution that tests and purifies faith as fire assays metal (Barnes, Calvin); "as though some strange thing happened," suffering for Christ normal and foretold, not to surprise (Henry). The verse displays the expected trial. The verse marks the surprise: not to be felt. The verse displays the trial: fiery. The verse displays the purpose: to prove. The verse displays the expectation: normal. The exhortation displays trial as the believer's expected lot. The verse displays the fiery: trial. The verse displays the strange: not so. The verse displays the proving: of faith. The call not to be surprised by the fiery trial displays the right expectation of suffering: believers are not to think the coming fiery ordeal strange, as though something unheard-of had befallen them — for persecution that tests and purifies faith is the normal, foretold lot of the Christian. The verse displays the expected fire, turned to rejoicing in Christ's sufferings (v.13).
13:
"But rejoice, inasmuch as ye are partakers of Christ's sufferings; that, when his glory shall be revealed, ye may be glad also with exceeding joy." The witnesses note the call to rejoice as partakers of Christ's sufferings. On "rejoice, inasmuch as ye are partakers of Christ's sufferings," the grammatical voices note the paradoxical joy. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation: "rejoice, inasmuch as (in the degree that) ye are partakers (sharers) of Christ's sufferings" — instead of being surprised or dismayed, believers are to rejoice that their sufferings make them sharers in Christ's own sufferings; to suffer with him is a privilege and ground of joy.

On "partakers of Christ's sufferings," the witnesses note the fellowship. Barnes and Calvin note that to suffer for Christ is to share in his sufferings — to be united with him in his afflictions; this fellowship in suffering is the path that leads to fellowship in his glory.

On "when his glory shall be revealed, ye may be glad also with exceeding joy," the puritan Henry notes the future joy: present suffering looks forward to the day "when his glory shall be revealed (at Christ's return in glory)," when the sufferers "may be glad also with exceeding (overflowing, rapturous) joy" — those who share his sufferings now will share his glory then, with surpassing joy; present trial is the seed of future rapture. The verse displays the call to rejoice as partakers of Christ's sufferings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the joy — "rejoice, inasmuch as ye are partakers (sharers) of Christ's sufferings," rejoicing that suffering makes them sharers in Christ's, a privilege (Barnes, Robertson); to suffer for Christ is to share his afflictions, united with him (Barnes, Calvin); "when his glory shall be revealed, ye may be glad with exceeding joy," sharers in his sufferings now sharing his glory then with surpassing joy (Henry). The verse displays the rejoicing sufferer. The verse marks the joy: rejoice. The verse displays the fellowship: Christ's sufferings. The verse displays the future: glory revealed. The verse displays the gladness: exceeding joy. The call displays suffering with Christ leading to joy in his glory. The verse displays the rejoicing: now. The verse displays the partaking: of sufferings. The verse displays the joy: exceeding. The call to rejoice as partakers of Christ's sufferings displays the transformation of trial into joy: believers are to rejoice that their sufferings make them sharers in Christ's own — that when his glory is revealed, they may rejoice with exceeding joy. The verse displays the joy in suffering, crowned by the resting Spirit of glory (v.14).
14:
"If ye be reproached for the name of Christ, happy are ye; for the spirit of glory and of God resteth upon you: on their part he is evil spoken of, but on your part he is glorified." The witnesses note the blessedness of being reproached for Christ, on whom the Spirit rests. On "if ye be reproached for the name of Christ, happy are ye," the grammatical voices note the beatitude. Barnes and Robertson note the blessing (echoing Matthew 5:11): "if ye be reproached (reviled, insulted) for the name of Christ, happy (blessed) are ye" — to be reviled because one bears Christ's name is not shame but blessedness; the reproach is honour.

On "the spirit of glory and of God resteth upon you," the witnesses note the resting Spirit. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of the blessing: "the spirit of glory and of God (the glorious Spirit of God, the Holy Spirit) resteth (reposes, abides) upon you" — the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of glory, rests on the persecuted believer, as the Shekinah glory rested of old; the divine presence and honour are with the reproached.

On "on their part he is evil spoken of, but on your part he is glorified," the grammatical-historical note the contrast: "on their part (by the persecutors) he (the Spirit, or Christ, or God) is evil spoken of (blasphemed), but on your part (by you) he is glorified" — the same Christ/Spirit blasphemed by the persecutors is glorified by the sufferers; the reproach the world casts is turned to God's glory in the believer. The verse displays the blessedness of being reproached for Christ, on whom the Spirit rests.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the beatitude — "if ye be reproached (reviled) for the name of Christ, happy (blessed) are ye," reproach for Christ's name a blessedness, an honour (Matthew 5:11, Barnes, Robertson); "the spirit of glory and of God resteth upon you," the Holy Spirit, the Spirit of glory, abiding on the persecuted (Barnes, Calvin); "on their part... evil spoken of, but on your part... glorified," the same Spirit/Christ blasphemed by persecutors, glorified by the sufferers (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed reproach. The verse marks the reproach: for Christ's name. The verse displays the blessing: happy. The verse displays the Spirit: of glory, resting. The verse displays the contrast: blasphemed and glorified. The beatitude displays the reproached honoured by the resting Spirit. The verse displays the reproach: borne. The verse displays the Spirit: resting. The verse displays the glory: God's. The blessedness of being reproached for Christ, on whom the Spirit rests, displays the honour of persecution: to be reviled for Christ's name is blessedness, for the Spirit of glory and of God rests on the sufferer — and the very Christ the persecutors blaspheme is glorified in the one who suffers. The verse displays the resting Spirit of glory, distinguishing suffering for Christ from suffering for crime (v.15).
15:
"But let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer, or as a busybody in other men's matters." The brief verse notes the warning not to suffer as a wrongdoer. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note the careful qualification of the blessing: "let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer (a general term for a criminal)" — the blessedness belongs only to those who suffer for righteousness; if one suffers for actual crimes, there is no blessing, only deserved punishment.

On the list of crimes, the witnesses note the disgrace. Barnes and Calvin note that to suffer as a murderer, thief, or evildoer is shameful and just; such suffering brings no honour but disgrace; the believer must take care that his suffering is for Christ, not for sin.

On "a busybody in other men's matters," the grammatical-historical note the added caution: "a busybody (allotriepiskopos — one who meddles in others' affairs, an officious intermeddler) in other men's matters" — alongside the gross crimes is named the lesser but real fault of meddling; the believer is not to bring suffering on himself by interfering in others' business; even this is to be avoided. The verse displays the warning not to suffer as a wrongdoer.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the distinction — "let none of you suffer as a murderer, or as a thief, or as an evildoer," the blessing only for righteous suffering, not for crime (Barnes, Robertson); suffering as a criminal shameful and deserved, no honour but disgrace (Barnes, Calvin); "a busybody in other men's matters," the added caution against meddling, even this to be avoided (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the crimes: murder, theft, evil. The verse displays the meddling: busybody. The verse displays the verdict: no blessing in crime. The verse displays the care: suffer for Christ. The warning displays suffering for sin bringing disgrace, not honour. The verse displays the murderer: not so. The verse displays the evildoer: shamed. The verse displays the busybody: warned. The warning not to suffer as a wrongdoer displays the careful boundary of the blessing: none is to suffer as a murderer, thief, evildoer, or even a meddling busybody — such suffering is deserved disgrace, not blessed; the believer must suffer only for Christ. The verse displays the boundary of blessed suffering, met by glorifying God as a Christian (v.16).
16:
"Yet if any man suffer as a Christian, let him not be ashamed; but let him glorify God on this behalf." The witnesses note that to suffer as a Christian is no shame but ground to glorify God. On "if any man suffer as a Christian, let him not be ashamed," the grammatical voices note the honourable name. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with v.15: "if any man suffer as a Christian (Christianos — one of the earliest uses of the name, perhaps first a term of reproach), let him not be ashamed" — to suffer for bearing the name "Christian" is no disgrace; the believer is not to be ashamed of the name or of suffering for it.

On "as a Christian," the witnesses note the name. Barnes and Calvin note that "Christian" was a name early applied to believers (Acts 11:26), perhaps in scorn by their enemies; to suffer under this name, far from being shameful, is an honour; the name marks one as Christ's.

On "let him glorify God on this behalf," the puritan Henry notes the right response: instead of shame, "let him glorify God on this behalf (in this name, or on this account)" — the believer is to praise and glorify God for the privilege of suffering as a Christian; the reproach becomes an occasion for thanksgiving. The verse displays that to suffer as a Christian is no shame but ground to glorify God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the honourable name — "if any man suffer as a Christian, let him not be ashamed," suffering for the name "Christian" no disgrace, not to be ashamed (Barnes, Robertson); "Christian" the name early applied (Acts 11:26), perhaps in scorn, but an honour to suffer under (Barnes, Calvin); "let him glorify God on this behalf," praising God for the privilege of suffering as a Christian (Henry). The verse displays the honoured name. The verse marks the suffering: as a Christian. The verse displays the shame: refused. The verse displays the response: glorify God. The verse displays the honour: in the name. The verse displays the believer glorying in the name of Christ. The verse displays the Christian: suffering. The verse displays the ashamed: not. The verse displays the glory: to God. That to suffer as a Christian is no shame but ground to glorify God displays the honour of the name: whoever suffers for bearing the name "Christian" is not to be ashamed, but to glorify God on that account — the reproach turned into thanksgiving. The verse displays the glorified God, as judgment begins at his house (v.17).
17:
"For the time is come that judgment must begin at the house of God: and if it first begin at us, what shall the end be of them that obey not the gospel of God?" The witnesses note that judgment begins at the house of God. On "the time is come that judgment must begin at the house of God," the grammatical voices note the order of judgment. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "the time is come that judgment (krima — the testing, sifting, chastening judgment) must begin at the house of God (the church, God's household)" — God's purifying judgment begins with his own people; the fiery trials come first upon the church, to sift and purify it.

On "judgment... at the house of God," the witnesses note the purifying chastisement. Barnes and Calvin note that this "judgment" is the disciplinary, purifying suffering God sends upon his own — not condemnation but chastening; the household of God is tried first, as a refiner begins with his own metal.

On "if it first begin at us, what shall the end be of them that obey not the gospel," the puritan Henry notes the solemn inference: "if it first begin at us (God's people), what shall the end be of them that obey not the gospel of God?" — if the righteous are subjected to such purifying trial, far more terrible will be the final judgment of the disobedient; the lesser severity to the saints implies a far greater to the lost. The verse displays that judgment begins at the house of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the order — "judgment (sifting, chastening) must begin at the house of God (the church)," God's purifying judgment beginning with his own (Barnes, Robertson); the "judgment" disciplinary and purifying, not condemnation, the household tried first (Barnes, Calvin); "if it first begin at us, what shall the end be of them that obey not the gospel," the lesser severity to the saints implying a far greater to the lost (Henry). The verse displays the beginning judgment. The verse marks the judgment: at God's house. The verse displays the purpose: purifying. The verse displays the inference: worse for the disobedient. The verse displays the order: saints first. The principle displays judgment starting with God's own and ending terribly for the lost. The verse displays the judgment: begun. The verse displays the house: of God. The verse displays the end: of the disobedient. That judgment begins at the house of God displays the order and warning of the divine judgment: the time has come for God's purifying judgment to begin with his own household — and if the righteous are so tried, how terrible will be the end of those who do not obey the gospel. The verse displays the judgment begun at God's house, scarcely saving the righteous (v.18).
18:
"And if the righteous scarcely be saved, where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear?" The brief verse notes that if the righteous are scarcely saved, the lost have no standing. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note the proverb (Proverbs 11:31, LXX): "if the righteous scarcely (with difficulty, through much trial) be saved, where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear?" — if even the righteous are saved only through great difficulty and trial, the ungodly will have no standing at all in the judgment.

On "the righteous scarcely be saved," the witnesses note the difficulty. Barnes and Calvin note that "scarcely" does not mean the righteous are saved by a narrow uncertainty as to the outcome, but that their salvation is attained through much trial, difficulty, and purifying suffering; the way is hard, the path narrow, salvation costly.

On "where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear," the grammatical-historical note the unanswerable question: "where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear (stand, be found)?" — the question expects no answer; if the godly are saved only with difficulty, the wicked have no hope of standing in the judgment; their case is desperate. The verse displays that if the righteous are scarcely saved, the lost have no standing.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the proverb (Proverbs 11:31) — "if the righteous scarcely (with difficulty, through trial) be saved, where shall the ungodly and the sinner appear?" the righteous saved only through great trial, the ungodly with no standing (Barnes, Robertson); "scarcely" meaning saved through much difficulty and purifying suffering, not narrow uncertainty (Barnes, Calvin); "where shall the ungodly... appear," the unanswerable question, the wicked having no hope of standing (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the costly salvation. The verse marks the righteous: scarcely saved. The verse displays the difficulty: through trial. The verse displays the question: where the ungodly. The verse displays the answer: no standing. The proverb displays the hard way of the saved and the hopeless case of the lost. The verse displays the righteous: scarcely. The verse displays the ungodly: undone. The verse displays the sinner: without standing. That if the righteous are scarcely saved, the lost have no standing displays the solemn force of the proverb: if even the godly attain salvation only through great trial, the ungodly and the sinner will have nowhere to stand in the judgment. The verse displays the desperate case of the lost, answered by the believer's committal to God (v.19).
19:
"Wherefore let them that suffer according to the will of God commit the keeping of their souls to him in well doing, as unto a faithful Creator." The witnesses note the call to commit one's soul to the faithful Creator in well doing. On "let them that suffer according to the will of God commit the keeping of their souls to him," the grammatical voices note the entrustment. Barnes and Robertson note the closing exhortation: "let them that suffer according to the will of God (those whose suffering is within God's appointment) commit (paratithesthosan — entrust, deposit, as with a trustee) the keeping of their souls to him" — the suffering believer is to entrust his soul to God for safekeeping, as one deposits a treasure with a faithful guardian.

On "in well doing," the witnesses note the active obedience. Barnes and Calvin note that the committal is to be joined with "well doing (continuing to do good)" — not a passive resignation only, but an active perseverance in doing right while suffering; faith and obedience together.

On "as unto a faithful Creator," the puritan Henry notes the ground of trust: the believer entrusts his soul "as unto a faithful (trustworthy, true to his promises) Creator" — God, as the one who made and sustains the soul, is faithful and will keep what is committed to him; his faithfulness and his creative care are the ground of confident entrustment. The verse displays the call to commit one's soul to the faithful Creator in well doing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the entrustment — "let them that suffer according to the will of God commit (entrust, deposit) the keeping of their souls to him," the sufferer entrusting his soul to God for safekeeping (Barnes, Robertson); "in well doing," joined with active perseverance in doing right, not passive resignation only (Barnes, Calvin); "as unto a faithful Creator," God faithful and able to keep what is committed, his creative care the ground of trust (Henry). The verse displays the committed soul. The verse marks the suffering: by God's will. The verse displays the committal: of the soul. The verse displays the action: well doing. The verse displays the ground: a faithful Creator. The call displays the sufferer entrusting his soul to the faithful God. The verse displays the soul: committed. The verse displays the well doing: continued. The verse displays the Creator: faithful. The call to commit one's soul to the faithful Creator in well doing displays the closing counsel of the chapter on suffering: those who suffer within God's will are to entrust their souls to him for safekeeping, while continuing to do good — resting on him as a faithful Creator who will keep what is committed to him. The verse displays the entrusted soul, closing the chapter on suffering as a Christian and the keeping of God.
